title: Writing - Part 1: Fast Food and Diet
description: Your class has been discussing the influence of fast food on people's eating habits.

content.question: Write an essay discussing two of the influences in your notes. You should explain which influence you think is more harmful, giving reasons for your opinion.

influences: Reducing time spent cooking at home
opinions: Fast food is a major cause of obesity in many countries.
People who cook their own food tend to eat more healthily.
Fast food companies deliberately target children with their advertising.

structure.introduction: Introduce the fast food industry and the two influences you will discuss.
structure.bodyParagraph1: Discuss the first influence (e.g., unhealthy ingredients) and how it affects health.
structure.bodyParagraph2: Discuss the second influence (e.g., reducing home cooking) and its consequences for diet.
structure.conclusion: Evaluate both influences and state which you consider more harmful.

content.modelAnswer:
Fast food has become increasingly popular across the world, with millions of people choosing burgers, pizza and fried chicken over home-cooked meals. While it offers convenience, its influence on people's diets has raised serious health concerns. This essay will discuss the effect of unhealthy ingredients and the decline of home cooking.

Many fast food products are high in fat, salt and sugar, providing large amounts of calories with very few essential nutrients. Regular consumption of such foods contributes to weight gain and increases the risk of serious illnesses, including heart disease and type 2 diabetes. Children and teenagers who eat fast food frequently are particularly vulnerable to these long-term health consequences.

Equally concerning is the way fast food culture has discouraged people from cooking at home. When ready meals and takeaways are cheap and easily available, fewer people take the time to prepare fresh, balanced meals. This means that cooking skills are being lost across generations, making families increasingly dependent on processed food.

In conclusion, while both influences are worrying, I believe the loss of home cooking skills is the more damaging long-term issue. When people cannot cook, they lose control over what they eat, making it far harder to maintain a healthy diet. Promoting cooking education in schools could be a vital step in reversing this trend.
